TL;DR: The paper introduces a new evaluation framework for encoding models in MEEG, using a novel metric (CPA-PA) that significantly improves sensitivity and robustness compared to existing approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation metrics for encoding models in MEEG are limited by the noisy nature of the data, where most variance is unrelated to sensory inputs. This paper aims to overcome the challenge of grounded evaluations by developing a method that approximates the ground-truth neural signal.

Method: The authors employ canonical correlation analysis (CCA) and participant averaging to align MEEG signals with model predictions, introducing the CPA-PA metric as a means to approximate the ground-truth neural activity for evaluation.

Result: The CPA-PA metric exhibited improved sensitivity and robustness, outperforming conventional evaluation metrics by 300-1000% on synthetic EEG data and 250% on real MEEG datasets.

Conclusion: Ground-truth approximation can serve as a robust and sensitive framework for evaluating encoding models in MEEG, overcoming limitations of traditional methods and advancing model interpretation and hypothesis testing.

Abstract: Encoding models enable measurement of how our brains represent sensory inputs using electro-and magneto-encephalography (MEEG). Evaluating how closely encoding models reflect the underlying brain functions is a crucial premise for model interpretation and hypothesis testing. However, the ground-truth neural activity is unknown, preventing model evaluation with respect to the target neural signal. Existing evaluation metrics must therefore relate model's predictions to noisy MEEG measurements, where most variance is stimulus-unrelated. Here, I introduce an evaluation framework where model predictions are compared to a ground-truth approximation, obtained by aligning MEEG signals with predictions using canonical correlation analysis and via participant averaging. The resulting metric (CPA-PA) yields single-participant evaluations outperforming conventional scores by 300-1000% on synthetic EEG data and 250% on 34 real MEEG datasets (818 datapoints). These gains reflect increased sensitivity to stimulus-relevant neural activity and reduced dependence on SNR, establishing ground-truth approximation as a robust framework for evaluating encoding models.

</details>


### [420] [Goxpyriment: A Go Framework for Behavioral and Cognitive Experiments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.15245)
*Christophe Pallier,Julie Bonnaire,Marie-France Fourcade*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: Goxpyriment is an open-source framework for programming behavioral and cognitive experiments in the Go language, offering simplified deployment, reliable timing, and AI-friendly experiment implementations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations in Python-based experiment tools, specifically challenges with runtime environment complexity and deployment issues across laboratories.

Method: Development of Goxpyriment, a Go-based library for creating self-contained executable binaries with assets embedded. Its interface is inspired by Expyriment, offering reliable timing features and a library of psychology experiments.

Result: Goxpyriment simplifies deployment, ensures accurate timing by leveraging OS-level timestamping, and enhances experiment programming with over 40 implementations. It also facilitates AI-enabled programming.

Conclusion: The framework provides a robust, user-friendly, and freely accessible solution for cognitive and behavioral experiment programming, addressing deployment and timing challenges faced by existing tools.

Abstract: We introduce `Goxpyriment', a new open-source software framework for programming behavioral and cognitive experiments using the Go programming language. The library is designed to address some limitations of existing Python-based experiment tools, particularly the runtime environment complexity that frequently complicates deployment across laboratories. Because Go is a compiled language that can natively embed assets (e.g., graphics, audio files, and stimulus lists), Goxpyriment compiles entire experiments into single, self-contained executable binaries with zero runtime dependencies. This drastically simplifies distribution to collaborators and testing computers. The programming interface, inspired by Expyriment (Krause & Lindemann, 2014), was designed to be human friendly. The library includes an array of visual stimuli (text, shapes, images, Gabor patches, motion clouds, ...) and audio capabilities (WAV playback and tone generation). While developing Goxpyriment, we focused on timing reliability. Input events are timestamped by the operating system at hardware-interrupt time, so reaction times are computed by subtracting two OS-level timestamps rather than relying on continuous polling. Go's garbage collector can be disabled, greatly reducing the probability of unpredictable pauses that could corrupt stimulus timing. Finally, a set of over forty psychology experiments implemented in Goxpyriment are provided that promote not only learning by humans but also improve the ability of modern AI-assisted coding tools to help program experiments. The framework is released under the
  GNU General Public License v3 and is freely available at https://github.com/chrplr/goxpyriment.

</details>


<div id='stat.ML'></div>

# stat.ML [[Back]](#toc)

### [421] [Doubly Outlier-Robust Online Infinite Hidden Markov Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14322)
*Horace Yiu,Leandro Sánchez-Betancourt,Álvaro Cartea,Gerardo Duran-Martin*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The authors propose a robust update rule for the online infinite hidden Markov model (iHMM) using generalized Bayesian inference, addressing issues like outliers and model misspecification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of online iHMMs when dealing with streaming data containing anomalies (outliers) and model misspecification, which often compromise reliability and accuracy.

Method: Introducing Batched Robust iHMM (BR-iHMM), which incorporates generalized Bayesian inference, uses posterior influence function (PIF) to quantify robustness, and introduces tunable parameters to balance adaptivity and robustness.

Result: BR-iHMM achieved up to a 67% reduction in one-step-ahead forecasting error on three datasets: limit order book data, hourly electricity demand, and synthetic high-dimensional systems.

Conclusion: BR-iHMM provides a practical, theoretically sound, and interpretable solution for robust and adaptive online learning in streaming data scenarios, with bounded PIF and reduced prediction errors.

Abstract: We derive a robust update rule for the online infinite hidden Markov model (iHMM) for when the streaming data contains outliers and the model is misspecified. Leveraging recent advances in generalised Bayesian inference, we define robustness via the posterior influence function (PIF), and provide conditions under which the online iHMM has bounded PIF. Imposing robustness inevitably induces an adaptation lag for regime switching. Our method, which is called Batched Robust iHMM (BR-iHMM), balances adaptivity and robustness with two additional tunable parameters. Across limit order book data, hourly electricity demand, and a synthetic high-dimensional linear system, BR-iHMM reduces one-step-ahead forecasting error by up to 67% relative to competing online Bayesian methods. Together with theoretical guarantees of bounded PIF, our results highlight the practicality of our approach for both forecasting and interpretable online learning.

</details>


### [422] [Differentially Private Conformal Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14621)
*Jiamei Wu,Ce Zhang,Zhipeng Cai,Jingsen Kong,Bei Jiang,Linglong Kong,Lingchen Kong*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes a method to combine conformal prediction (CP) with differential privacy (DP) for efficient and private uncertainty quantification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study addresses the inefficiency of conformal prediction when combined with privacy constraints, aiming to improve statistical and computational efficiency under differential privacy.

Method: It introduces a new method called Differentially Private Conformal Prediction (DPCP), combining DP model training with a private quantile mechanism for calibration, alongside the analytic concept of Differential CP.

Result: The paper shows that DPCP creates tighter prediction sets compared to existing private split conformal approaches, maintaining privacy guarantees, validated through experiments on synthetic and real data.

Conclusion: The proposed methods successfully balance statistical efficiency and privacy, filling a critical gap in uncertainty quantification under DP constraints while outperforming existing approaches.

Abstract: Conformal prediction (CP) has attracted broad attention as a simple and flexible framework for uncertainty quantification through prediction sets. In this work, we study how to deploy CP under differential privacy (DP) in a statistically efficient manner. We first introduce differential CP, a non-splitting conformal procedure that avoids the efficiency loss caused by data splitting and serves as a bridge between oracle CP and private conformal inference. By exploiting the stability properties of DP mechanisms, differential CP establishes a direct connection to oracle CP and inherits corresponding validity behavior. Building on this idea, we develop Differentially Private Conformal Prediction (DPCP), a fully private procedure that combines DP model training with a private quantile mechanism for calibration. We establish the end-to-end privacy guarantee of DPCP and investigate its coverage properties under additional regularity conditions. We further study the efficiency of both differential CP and DPCP under empirical risk minimization and general regression models, showing that DPCP can produce tighter prediction sets than existing private split conformal approaches under the same privacy budget. Numerical experiments on synthetic and real datasets demonstrate the practical effectiveness of the proposed methods.

</details>


### [423] [Expert-Guided Class-Conditional Goodness-of-Fit Scores for Interpretable Classification with Informative Missingness: An Application to Seismic Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14809)
*Shahar Cohen,David M. Steinberg,Yael Radzyner,Yochai Ben Horin*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes an interpretable classification framework that integrates expert knowledge and handles missing data efficiently, particularly for seismic monitoring applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in classification tasks involving missing data, incorporating expert knowledge, and ensuring interpretability of decision rules.

Method: The framework uses expert-defined class-conditional models to construct interpretable features, integrates auxiliary summaries, and develops a simple discriminative classifier.

Result: The proposed framework proved effective for seismic monitoring under the Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty, enhancing interpretability and outperforming standard machine-learning models in small-sample scenarios.

Conclusion: The method serves as a useful, interpretable screening tool for complex domains, showing potential to reduce expert workloads while maintaining high classification performance.

Abstract: We study a classification problem with three key challenges: pervasive informative missingness, the integration of partial prior expert knowledge into the learning process, and the need for interpretable decision rules. We propose a framework that encodes prior knowledge through an expert-guided class-conditional model for one or more classes, and use this model to construct a small set of interpretable goodness-of-fit features. The features quantify how well the observed data agree with the expert model, isolating the contributions of different aspects of the data, including both observed and missing components. These features are combined with a few transparent auxiliary summaries in a simple discriminative classifier, resulting in a decision rule that is easy to inspect and justify. We develop and apply the framework in the context of seismic monitoring used to assess compliance with the Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty. We show that the method has strong potential as a transparent screening tool, reducing workload for expert analysts. A simulation designed to isolate the contribution of the proposed framework shows that this interpretable expert-guided method can even outperform strong standard machine-learning classifiers, particularly when training samples are small.

</details>


### [424] [Scalable Model-Based Clustering with Sequential Monte Carlo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14810)
*Connie Trojan,Pavel Myshkov,Paul Fearnhead,James Hensman,Tom Minka,Christopher Nemeth*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a scalable Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) algorithm for handling clustering problems under uncertainty, especially with complex distributions, by decomposing them into manageable subproblems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of clustering with high uncertainty and complex distributions, such as in text data, where traditional SMC struggles with scalability.

Method: A novel SMC algorithm that breaks down clustering problems into approximately independent subproblems, reducing memory requirements while maintaining accuracy.

Result: The proposed method demonstrated accurate and efficient clustering performance for knowledge base construction and similar scenarios where typical SMC fails.

Conclusion: The approach effectively strikes a balance between scalability and precision, making SMC practical for large-scale clustering tasks under uncertainty.

Abstract: In online clustering problems, there is often a large amount of uncertainty over possible cluster assignments that cannot be resolved until more data are observed. This difficulty is compounded when clusters follow complex distributions, as is the case with text data. Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) methods give a natural way of representing and updating this uncertainty over time, but have prohibitive memory requirements for large-scale problems. We propose a novel SMC algorithm that decomposes clustering problems into approximately independent subproblems, allowing a more compact representation of the algorithm state. Our approach is motivated by the knowledge base construction problem, and we show that our method is able to accurately and efficiently solve clustering problems in this setting and others where traditional SMC struggles.

</details>


### [425] [Best of both worlds: Stochastic & adversarial best-arm identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14860)
*Yasin Abbasi-Yadkori,Peter L. Bartlett,Victor Gabillon,Alan Malek,Michal Valko*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper explores the problem of identifying the best arm in bandit settings with either stochastic or adversarial rewards. The authors show that no single learner can perform optimally across both scenarios, provide a lower bound for stochastic problems under adversarial constraints, and propose a parameter-free algorithm achieving robust and near-optimal performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of designing a learner that performs optimally in both adversarial and stochastic bandit best-arm identification problems without prior knowledge of the reward nature.

Method: The authors analyze theoretical limits of best-arm identification under mixed reward scenarios. They derive a lower bound for stochastic problems constrained by adversarial robustness and introduce a parameter-free algorithm that aligns with this bound and ensures dual robustness.

Result: The proposed algorithm achieves optimal rates of error (up to logarithmic factors) in stochastic problems, while maintaining robustness to adversarial rewards.

Conclusion: Although achieving absolute optimality in both adversarial and stochastic settings is impossible, the proposed parameter-free algorithm balances performance effectively across both reward types.

Abstract: We study bandit best-arm identification with arbitrary and potentially adversarial rewards. A simple random uniform learner obtains the optimal rate of error in the adversarial scenario. However, this type of strategy is suboptimal when the rewards are sampled stochastically. Therefore, we ask: Can we design a learner that performs optimally in both the stochastic and adversarial problems while not being aware of the nature of the rewards? First, we show that designing such a learner is impossible in general. In particular, to be robust to adversarial rewards, we can only guarantee optimal rates of error on a subset of the stochastic problems. We give a lower bound that characterizes the optimal rate in stochastic problems if the strategy is constrained to be robust to adversarial rewards. Finally, we design a simple parameter-free algorithm and show that its probability of error matches (up to log factors) the lower bound in stochastic problems, and it is also robust to adversarial ones.

</details>


### [426] [Unsupervised feature selection using Bayesian Tucker decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14949)
*Y-h. Taguchi,Yoh-ichi Mototake*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces Bayesian Tucker decomposition (BTuD) for Gaussian-distributed residuals, enabling unsupervised feature selection successfully demonstrated across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a novel unsupervised feature selection method leveraging Tucker decomposition for broader applicability.

Method: The authors proposed Bayesian Tucker decomposition (BTuD), implemented through higher-order orthogonal iteration, using Gaussian residual assumptions.

Result: BTuD effectively performed unsupervised feature selection across synthetic datasets, global maps with variable coupling, and gene expression profiles.

Conclusion: BTuD is a promising method for unsupervised feature selection, aligning well with prior Tucker decomposition-based approaches.

Abstract: In this paper, we proposed Bayesian Tucker decomposition (BTuD) in which residual is supposed to obey Gaussian distribution analogous to linear regression. Although we have proposed an algorithm to perform the proposed BTuD, the conventional higher-order orthogonal iteration can generate Tucker decomposition consistent with the present implementation. Using the proposed BTuD, we can perform unsupervised feature selection successfully applied to various synthetic datasets, global coupled maps with randomized coupling strength, and gene expression profiles. Thus we can conclude that our newly proposed unsupervised feature selection method is promising. In addition to this, BTuD based unsupervised FE is expected to coincide with TD based unsupervised FE that were previously proposed and successfully applied to a wide range of problems.

</details>


### [427] [MinShap: A Modified Shapley Value Approach for Feature Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.15107)
*Chenghui Zheng,Garvesh Raskutti*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper focuses on adapting Shapley values for improved feature selection in statistical and machine learning models, introducing MinShap for direct and stable evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing feature selection methods struggle with unknown non-linear relationships and dependent features. Shapley values, while effective for feature attribution in such scenarios, aren't directly suitable for feature selection due to their incorporation of indirect effects.

Method: The authors propose MinShap, a modified Shapley value framework that uses minimum marginal contributions across permutations instead of averages. Additional algorithms utilizing multiple testing/p-value perspectives are introduced to enhance performance in settings with lower sample sizes.

Result: MinShap demonstrated superior accuracy and stability compared to established methods like LOCO, GCM, and Lasso, confirmed through theoretical guarantees, simulations, and real data experiments.

Conclusion: MinShap and related methods provide a robust solution for feature selection, combining accuracy, stability, and theoretical reliability, particularly in complex models involving non-linear and dependent feature interactions.

Abstract: Feature selection is a classical problem in statistics and machine learning, and it continues to remain an extremely challenging problem especially in the context of unknown non-linear relationships with dependent features. On the other hand, Shapley values are a classic solution concept from cooperative game theory that is widely used for feature attribution in general non-linear models with highly-dependent features. However, Shapley values are not naturally suited for feature selection since they tend to capture both direct effects from each feature to the response and indirect effects through other features. In this paper, we combine the advantages of Shapley values and adapt them to feature selection by proposing \emph{MinShap}, a modification of the Shapley value framework along with a suite of other related algorithms. In particular for MinShap, instead of taking the average marginal contributions over permutations of features, considers the minimum marginal contribution across permutations. We provide a theoretical foundation motivated by the faithfulness assumption in DAG (directed acyclic graphical models), a guarantee for the Type I error of MinShap, and show through numerical simulations and real data experiments that MinShap tends to outperform state-of-the-art feature selection algorithms such as LOCO, GCM and Lasso in terms of both accuracy and stability. We also introduce a suite of algorithms related to MinShap by using the multiple testing/p-value perspective that improves performance in lower-sample settings and provide supporting theoretical guarantees.

</details>


### [428] [Amortized Optimal Transport from Sliced Potentials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.15114)
*Minh-Phuc Truong,Khai Nguyen*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper presents two amortized optimization methods, RA-OT and OA-OT, that predict optimal transport plans efficiently by leveraging Kantorovich potentials through sliced OT. These approaches enable quick approximations for repeated OT problems and demonstrate strong performance across various tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Solving optimal transport problems repeatedly across multiple measure pairs is computationally expensive. The authors aim to make these solutions efficient by reusing learned information.

Method: Two strategies are introduced: regression-based amortization (RA-OT), which uses functional regression, and objective-based amortization (OA-OT), which optimizes the Kantorovich dual objective. Both leverage sliced OT for parsimonious and accurate predictions.

Result: The proposed methods achieve high accuracy and efficiency across diverse tasks, including MNIST digit transport, spherical data transportation, and conditional flow matching.

Conclusion: RA-OT and OA-OT provide faster and generalized solutions to repeated OT problems by reusing prior knowledge, with added benefits of independence from specific measure structures and promising results in practical applications.

Abstract: We propose a novel amortized optimization method for predicting optimal transport (OT) plans across multiple pairs of measures by leveraging Kantorovich potentials derived from sliced OT. We introduce two amortization strategies: regression-based amortization (RA-OT) and objective-based amortization (OA-OT). In RA-OT, we formulate a functional regression model that treats Kantorovich potentials from the original OT problem as responses and those obtained from sliced OT as predictors, and estimate these models via least-squares methods. In OA-OT, we estimate the parameters of the functional model by optimizing the Kantorovich dual objective. In both approaches, the predicted OT plan is subsequently recovered from the estimated potentials. As amortized OT methods, both RA-OT and OA-OT enable efficient solutions to repeated OT problems across different measure pairs by reusing information learned from prior instances to rapidly approximate new solutions. Moreover, by exploiting the structure provided by sliced OT, the proposed models are more parsimonious, independent of specific structures of the measures, such as the number of atoms in the discrete case, while achieving high accuracy. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approaches on tasks including MNIST digit transport, color transfer, supply-demand transportation on spherical data, and mini-batch OT conditional flow matching.

</details>


### [429] [Structural interpretability in SVMs with truncated orthogonal polynomial kernels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.15285)
*Víctor Soto-Larrosa,Nuria Torrado,Edmundo J. Huertas*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method, ORCA, to analyze the interpretability of SVMs with truncated orthogonal polynomial kernels, revealing detailed structural insights beyond predictive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to enhance post-training interpretability of SVM classifiers, leveraging the explicit representation properties of truncated orthogonal polynomial kernels.

Method: They develop Orthogonal Representation Contribution Analysis (ORCA), which uses normalized OKC indices to dissect contributions in RKHS coordinates across interaction orders, polynomial degrees, and marginal effects without retraining.

Result: ORCA is demonstrated on synthetic and real datasets, showing its ability to uncover model complexity aspects not reflected by accuracy metrics.

Conclusion: The proposed framework is useful for diagnostically evaluating SVM classifiers, providing rich insights about interaction, effects, and contributions in a post-training setting.

Abstract: We study post-training interpretability for Support Vector Machines (SVMs) built from truncated orthogonal polynomial kernels. Since the associated reproducing kernel Hilbert space is finite-dimensional and admits an explicit tensor-product orthonormal basis, the fitted decision function can be expanded exactly in intrinsic RKHS coordinates. This leads to Orthogonal Representation Contribution Analysis (ORCA), a diagnostic framework based on normalized Orthogonal Kernel Contribution (OKC) indices. These indices quantify how the squared RKHS norm of the classifier is distributed across interaction orders, total polynomial degrees, marginal coordinate effects, and pairwise contributions. The methodology is fully post-training and requires neither surrogate models nor retraining. We illustrate its diagnostic value on a synthetic double-spiral problem and on a real five-dimensional echocardiogram dataset. The results show that the proposed indices reveal structural aspects of model complexity that are not captured by predictive accuracy alone.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [430] [Exploiting Correlations in Federated Learning: Opportunities and Practical Limitations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14751)
*Adrian Edin,Michel Kieffer,Mikael Johansson,Zheng Chen*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper classifies and analyzes gradient and model compression schemes in federated learning based on structural, temporal, and spatial correlations, recommending adaptive designs for better results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the communication bottleneck in federated learning and reduce data exchange between clients and the parameter server.

Method: Systematic classification of compression schemes, proposing metrics to measure correlations, and introducing adaptive designs that switch modes based on correlation strength.

Result: Experiments show significant variation in correlation degrees based on task, model, and configurations; adaptive designs outperform traditional non-adaptive approaches.

Conclusion: A unified taxonomy enables more effective, tailored compression techniques for federated learning systems.

Abstract: The communication bottleneck in federated learning (FL) has spurred extensive research into techniques to reduce the volume of data exchanged between client devices and the central parameter server. In this paper, we systematically classify gradient and model compression schemes into three categories based on the type of correlations they exploit: structural, temporal, and spatial. We examine the sources of such correlations, propose quantitative metrics for measuring their magnitude, and reinterpret existing compression methods through this unified correlation-based framework. Our experimental studies demonstrate that the degrees of structural, temporal, and spatial correlations vary significantly depending on task complexity, model architecture, and algorithmic configurations. These findings suggest that algorithm designers should carefully evaluate correlation assumptions under specific deployment scenarios rather than assuming that they are always present. Motivated by these findings, we propose two adaptive compression designs that actively switch between different compression modes based on the measured correlation strength, and we evaluate their performance gains relative to conventional non-adaptive approaches. In summary, our unified taxonomy provides a clean and principled foundation for developing more effective and application-specific compression techniques for FL systems.

</details>


### [431] [A Synonymous Variational Perspective on the Rate-Distortion-Perception Tradeoff](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14603)
*Zijian Liang,Kai Niu,Changshuo Wang,Jin Xu,Ping Zhang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper combines rate-distortion theory with a semantic-based framework for signal compression and reconstructs signals using a synonymous set approach, enabling new theoretical insights into perceptual optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the unclear theoretical origin of perceptual quality measures in the rate-distortion-perception framework by introducing a semantic-driven synonymous reconstruction perspective.

Method: Develops a synonymous variational inference framework and synonymous variational lower bound to analyze compression based on reconstructing signals from synonymous sets instead of specific samples.

Result: Derives the synonymity-perception consistency principle, establishes a synonymous rate-distortion-perception tradeoff, and clarifies the theoretical basis for divergence-based perceptual optimization within compression.

Conclusion: Synonymous source coding aligns semantic information reconstruction with perceptual optimization, creating a theoretically consistent framework that integrates classical rate-distortion theory with perception-driven compression methods.

Abstract: The fundamental limit of natural signal compression has traditionally been characterized by classical rate-distortion (RD) theory through the tradeoff between coding rate and reconstruction distortion, while the rate-distortion-perception (RDP) framework introduces a divergence-based measure of perceptual quality as a modeling principle rather than a theoretically-derived principle, leaving its theoretical origin unclear. In this paper, motivated by a synonymity-based semantic information perspective, we reformulate perceptual reconstruction as recovering any admissible sample within an ideal synonymous set (synset) associated with the source, rather than the source sample itself, and correspondingly establish a synonymous source coding architecture. On this basis, we develop a synonymous variational inference (SVI) analysis framework with a synonymous variational lower bound (SVLBO) for tractable analysis of synset-oriented compression. Within this framework, we establish a synonymity-perception consistency principle, showing that optimal identification of semantic information is theoretically consistent with perceptual optimization. Based on its derivation result, we prove a synonymous RDP tradeoff for the proposed synonymous source coding. These analytical results show that the distributional divergence term arises naturally from the synset-based reconstruction objective, clarify its compatibility with existing RDP formulations and classical RD theory, and suggest the potential advantages of synonymous source coding.

</details>


### [432] [Regret Tail Characterization of Optimal Bandit Algorithms with Generic Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14876)
*Subhodip Panda,Shubhada Agrawal*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper studies regret-tail behaviors in stochastic multi-armed bandits, extending the KL-UCB algorithm to nonparametric reward settings and achieving tight guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the heavy-tail behavior of regret in bandit algorithms that are optimal in expectation but incur large regrets with non-negligible probabilities.

Method: The paper extends the KL-UCB algorithm to nonparametric reward settings under mild assumptions, analyzes its regret-tail behavior, and derives novel upper bounds on regret-tail probabilities.

Result: Regret-tail guarantees are provided for bounded-support and heavy-tailed bandit models, recovering known results and matching lower bounds for finitely-supported reward distributions.

Conclusion: The work offers a unified and sharp characterization of regret-tail probability for KL-based UCB algorithms, demonstrating their applicability beyond parametric models.

Abstract: We study the tail behavior of regret in stochastic multi-armed bandits for algorithms that are asymptotically optimal in expectation. While minimizing expected regret is the classical objective, recent work shows that even such algorithms can exhibit heavy regret tails, incurring large regret with non-negligible probability. Existing sharp characterizations of regret tails are largely restricted to parametric settings, such as single-parameter exponential families.
  In this work, we extend the $\KLinf$-UCB algorithm of to a broad nonparametric class of reward distributions satisfying mild assumptions, and establish its asymptotic optimality in expectation. We then analyze the tail behavior of its regret and derive a novel upper bound on the regret tail probability. As special cases, our results recover regret-tail guarantees for both bounded-support and heavy-tailed (moment-bounded) bandit models. Moreover, for the special case of finitely-supported reward distributions, our upper bound matches the known lower bound exactly. Our results thus provide a unified and tight characterization of regret tails for asymptotically optimal KL-based UCB algorithms, going beyond parametric models.

</details>


### [433] [Aerial Multi-Functional RIS in Fluid Antennas-Aided Full-Duplex Networks: A Self-Optimized Hybrid Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14309)
*Li-Hsiang Shen,Yu-Quan Zheng*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper proposes a novel system combining autonomous aerial vehicles (AAVs), multi-functional reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (AM-RIS), and fluid antennas (FA) in full-duplex (FD) networks, maximizing energy efficiency using a hybrid deep reinforcement learning framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address high data traffic demands and energy efficiency challenges in sixth-generation (6G) wireless networks by integrating innovative technologies such as AM-RIS, FA, and FD capabilities.

Method: The authors developed an architecture incorporating AM-RIS and FA in FD networks and designed a self-optimized multi-agent hybrid deep reinforcement learning (SOHRL) framework for optimizing beamforming, user power, AM-RIS configuration, and positioning.

Result: Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed system, showing that SOHRL outperforms other methods and that AM-RIS achieves the highest energy efficiency compared to alternative setups.

Conclusion: AM-RIS-enabled FA-aided FD networks powered by SOHRL present a promising solution for energy-efficiency-oriented 6G wireless networks, surpassing existing benchmarks and conventional approaches.

Abstract: To address high data traffic demands of sixth-generation (6G) networks, this paper proposes a novel architecture that integrates autonomous aerial vehicles (AAVs) and multi-functional reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (MF-RISs) as AM-RIS in fluid antenna (FA)-assisted full-duplex (FD) networks. The AM-RIS provides hybrid functionalities, including signal reflection, amplification, and energy harvesting (EH), potentially improving both signal coverage and sustainability. Meanwhile, FA facilitates fine-grained spatial adaptability at FD-enabled base station (BS), which complements residual self-interference (SI) suppression. We aim at maximizing the overall energy efficiency (EE) by jointly optimizing transmit DL beamforming at BS, UL user power, configuration of AM-RIS, and positions of the FA and AM-RIS. Owing to the hybrid continuous-discrete parameters and high dimensionality of the intractable problem, we have conceived a self-optimized multi-agent hybrid deep reinforcement learning (DRL) framework (SOHRL), which integrates multi-agent deep Q-networks (DQN) and multi-agent proximal policy optimization (PPO), respectively handling discrete and continuous actions. To enhance self-adaptability, an attention-driven state representation and meta-level hyperparameter optimization are incorporated, enabling multi-agents to autonomously adjust learning hyperparameters. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed AM-RIS-enabled FA-aided FD networks empowered by SOHRL algorithm. The results reveal that SOHRL outperforms benchmarks of the case without attention mechanism and conventional hybrid/multi-agent/standalone DRL. Moreover, AM-RIS in FD achieves the highest EE compared to half-duplex, conventional rigid antenna arrays, partial EH, and conventional RIS without amplification, highlighting its potential as a compelling solution for EE-aware wireless networks.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [434] [Bias in Surface Electromyography Features across a Demographically Diverse Cohort](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14460)
*Aditi Agrawal,Celine John Philip,Giancarlo K. Sagastume,Marcus A. Battraw,Wilsaan M. Joiner,Jonathon S. Schofield,Lee M. Miller,Richard S. Whittle*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper examines how demographic factors, such as age, sex, and body characteristics, influence signals in surface electromyography (sEMG), showing that a significant number of sEMG features are affected.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current sEMG systems suffer from inconsistent performance due to individual differences, making personalization labor-intensive and limiting their accessibility for diverse populations.

Method: Researchers used mixed-effects linear models and partial least squares (PLS) analysis to investigate associations between sEMG features and demographic variables, analyzing data from 81 individuals performing hand gestures.

Result: 33% of commonly used sEMG features were significantly associated with demographic characteristics. This finding highlights biases that could impact the functionality of sEMG-based applications.

Conclusion: The study suggests that demographic differences need to be accounted for in sEMG-based neural interfaces, paving the way for fairer and more universally deployable systems.

Abstract: Neuromotor decoding from upper-limb electromyography (sEMG) can enhance human-machine interfaces and offer a more natural means of controlling prosthetic limbs, virtual reality, and household electronics. Unfortunately, current sEMG technology does not always perform consistently across users because individual differences such as age and body mass index, among many others, can substantially alter signal quality. This variability makes sEMG characteristics highly idiosyncratic, often necessitating laborious personalization and iterative tuning to achieve reliable performance. This variability has particular import for sEMG-based assistive devices and neural interfaces, where demographic biases in sEMG features could undermine broad and fair deployment.
  In this study, we explore how demographic differences affect the sEMG signals produced and their implications for machine learning-based gesture decoding. We analyze the data set provided by, in which we derive 147 common sEMG features extracted from 81 demographically diverse individuals performing discrete hand gestures. Using mixed-effects linear models and partial least squares (PLS) analysis, which take into consideration demographic variables (including age, sex, height, weight, skin properties, subcutaneous fat, and hair density), we identify that 33\% (49 of 147) of commonly used sEMG features show significant associations with demographic characteristics. These results may help guide the development of fair and unbiased sEMG-based neural interfaces across a diverse population.

</details>


### [435] [Low-Cost System for Automatic Recognition of Driving Pattern in Assessing Interurban Mobility using Geo-Information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.15216)
*Oscar Romero,Aika Silveira Miura,Lorena Parra,Jaime Lloret*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper explores a system to assess and enhance driving safety using sensors, an artificial neural network for recognizing driving styles, and auditory feedback. The approach significantly improves accuracy, with up to 92% achieved for dual-style classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To mitigate traffic jams and accidents caused by unsafe driving styles, especially for vehicles without pre-installed driving evaluation systems.

Method: The proposed system uses two physical sensors connected to a device node with an artificial neural network. This system collects data such as velocity, position, and turning speed to recognize driving styles and provide real-time feedback.

Result: The system demonstrated an accuracy of 83% when recognizing three driving styles, and 92% when limited to two styles (normal and aggressive). Including geo-information and time data improved classification accuracy by 13%.

Conclusion: The prototype demonstrates the feasibility of using sensor data and neural networks to improve driving style assessment and promote safer and more efficient driving habits.

Abstract: Mobility in urban and interurban areas, mainly by cars, is a day-to-day activity of many people. However, some of its main drawbacks are traffic jams and accidents. Newly made vehicles have pre-installed driving evaluation systems, which can prevent accidents. However, most cars on our roads do not have driver assessment systems. In this paper, we propose an approach for recognising driving styles and enabling drivers to reach safer and more efficient driving. The system consists of two physical sensors connected to a device node with a display and a speaker. An artificial neural network (ANN) is included in the node, which analyses the data from the sensors, and then recognises the driving style. When an abnormal driving pattern is detected, the speaker will play a warning message. The prototype was assembled and tested using an interurban road, in particular on a conventional road with three driving styles. The gathered data were used to train and validate the ANN. Results, in terms of accuracy, indicate that better accuracy is obtained when the velocity, position (latitude and longitude), time, and turning speed for the 3-axis are used, offering an average accuracy of 83%. If the classification is performed considering just two driving styles, normal and aggressive, then the accuracy reaches 92%. When the geo-information and time data are included, the main novelty of this paper, the classification accuracy is improved by 13%.

</details>


### [436] [FocalLens: Visualizing Narratives through Focalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14456)
*S M Raihanul Alam,Md Dilshadur Rahman,Md Naimul Hoque*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper introduces FocalLens, a visualization tool emphasizing focalization in narratives to help writers and scholars analyze and reflect on complex narrative components effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Currently available narrative visualizations fail to represent complex components like focalization, causality, and speech, which limits writers and scholars in identifying nuanced patterns and biases in narratives.

Method: The authors developed FocalLens, a visualization framework based on focalization, incorporating the perceptions, participation, observation, and narration of characters in narrative events. They also created a tool enabling interaction between text and the visualization and conducted a qualitative evaluation with writers and scholars.

Result: FocalLens provided unique insights into narrative structures, enabling writers to review drafts and scholars to analyze known texts effectively during the study. It added dimensions not accessible in their standard analytical workflow.

Conclusion: The study highlights the tool's value and suggests design implications, advocating further exploration in narrative visualization for capturing complex narrative elements.

Abstract: Visualizing narratives is useful to writers to reflect on unfinished drafts and identify unintentional biases and inconsistencies. Literary scholars can use the visualizations to identify nuanced patterns and literary styles from written text. Current narrative visualization is limited to representing character and location co-occurrences in a timeline, omitting important and complex narrative components such as focalization, causality, and speech. This paper aims to capture and visualize underexplored, complex narrative components as a basis for narrative visualization. As a starting point, we propose a new narrative visualization, named FocalLens, that uses focalization, the component that establishes who sees or perceives the events in a narrative, for representing the narrative. We provide the theoretical foundation of focalization and describe various types and facets of focalization. The details are incorporated in the novel visualization that captures how different characters perceive an event, who directly participate in an event, who indirectly observe the event, and who narrate the event. We also developed a tool that provides fluid interaction between the text and the proposed visualization. The tool was evaluated with four writers and scholars in a qualitative study, where writers analyzed their draft stories and scholars analyzed well-known stories. The findings suggest the tool added a new dimension to the workflow for writers and scholars, an analytical lens that is not available otherwise. We conclude by identifying design implications and future directions.

</details>


### [437] [Agentic Explainability at Scale: Between Corporate Fears and XAI Needs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14984)
*Yomna Elsayed,Cecily Jones*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper investigates the risks of scaling agentic AI adoption, particularly focusing on governance gaps and the phenomenon of "Agent Sprawl." It introduces design-time and runtime explainability techniques and proposes a prototype solution called the Agentic AI Card.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation behind this paper arises from the increasing adoption of agentic AI in enterprises, which raises concerns about risks like autonomy and lack of proper governance—especially when tools scale without corresponding oversight mechanisms.

Method: The authors conducted an exploration of AI governance professionals' concerns regarding the adoption of agentic AI. They also propose practical techniques, including design-time and runtime explainability strategies, and develop a prototype called the Agentic AI Card.

Result: The paper identifies significant gaps in agentic AI governance, particularly around observability and decision-making transparency of agents. It offers techniques and tools to address these governance challenges.

Conclusion: The paper emphasizes the critical need for robust governance strategies as businesses scale their usage of agentic AI. The proposed Agentic AI Card aims to bring clarity and comfort to companies deploying these tools at scale, thereby mitigating potential risks.

Abstract: As companies enter the race for agentic AI adoption, fears surface around agentic autonomy and its subsequent risks. These fears compound as companies scale their agentic AI adoption with low-code applications, without a comparable scaling in their governance processes and expertise resulting in a phenomenon known as "Agent Sprawl". While shadow AI tools can help with agentic discovery and identification, few observability tools offer insights into the agents' configuration and settings or the decision-making process during agent-to-agent communication and orchestration. This paper explores AI governance professionals' concerns in enterprise settings, while offering design-time and runtime explainability techniques as suggested by AI governance experts for addressing those fears. Finally, we provide a preliminary prototype of an Agentic AI Card that can help companies feel at ease deploying agents at scale.

</details>


### [438] [CoGrid & the Multi-User Gymnasium: A Framework for Multi-Agent Experimentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.15044)
*Chase McDonald,Cleotilde Gonzalez*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper introduces CoGrid and Multi-User Gymnasium (MUG), tools to facilitate multi-agent experiments involving humans and AI for studying social decision-making.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the lack of accessible tools for researchers to study human-AI interaction in multi-agent setups, which is important for understanding social decision-making.

Method: CoGrid provides a grid-based simulation library, while MUG transforms these simulations into interactive web-based experiments with support for multiple humans and AIs, using advanced networking techniques.

Result: The tools enable deployment of human-AI studies and support research in psychology, cognition, and decision-making through open-source platforms.

Conclusion: The availability of these tools reduces barriers for researchers and contributes to advancing the exploration of human-AI interactions in multi-agent frameworks.

Abstract: The increasing integration of artificial intelligence (AI) in everyday life brings with it new challenges and questions for regarding how humans interact with autonomous agents. Multi-agent experiments, where humans and AI act together, can offer important opportunities to study social decision making, but there is a lack of accessible tooling available to researchers to run such experiments. We introduce two tools designed to reduce these barriers. The first, CoGrid, is a multi-agent grid-based simulation library with dual NumPy and JAX backends. The second, Multi-User Gymnasium (MUG), translates such simulation environments directly into interactive web-based experiments. MUG supports interactions with arbitrary numbers of humans and AI, utilizing either server-authoritative or peer-to-peer networking with rollback netcode to account for latency. Together, these tools can enable researchers to deploy studies of human-AI interaction, facilitating inquiry into core questions of psychology, cognition, and decision making and their relationship to human-AI interaction. Both tools are open source and available to the broader research community. Documentation and source code is available at {cogrid, multi-user-gymnasium}.readthedocs.io. This paper details the functionality of these tools and presents several case studies to illustrate their utility in human-AI multi-agent experimentation.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.BM'></div>

# q-bio.BM [[Back]](#toc)

### [439] [Polyformer: a generative framework for thermodynamic modeling of polymeric molecules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14241)
*Alessio Valentini,David Pekker,Chungwen Liang,Todd Martinez,Swagatam Mukhopadhyay*

Main category: q-bio.BM

TL;DR: This paper presents Polyformer, a generative model predicting the thermodynamic conformational ensemble of biomolecules based on sequence and temperature.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations in static models like AlphaFold by enabling dynamic predictions of biomolecular conformational ensembles crucial for biological function.

Method: Polyformer leverages a generative framework to compute conformational ensembles using sequence and thermodynamic variables like temperature.

Result: The model successfully predicts protein domain conformational ensembles, with results aligning well with Molecular Dynamics simulations.

Conclusion: Polyformer advances structural biology by modeling dynamism in biomolecular conformations, bridging gaps unaddressed by existing models like AlphaFold.

Abstract: The classic paradigm of structural biology is that the sequence of a biomolecule (protein, nucleic acid, lipid, etc) determines its conformation (shape) which determines its biological function. Protein folding programs like AlphaFold address this paradigm by predicting the single best conformation given a sequence that defines the molecule. However, biomolecules are not static structures, and their conformational ensemble determines their function. We present the Polyformer -- a generative framework for thermodynamic modeling of polymeric molecules. Given the sequence and temperature (or another thermodynamic variable), the Polyformer generates conformations faithful to the molecule's thermodynamic conformational ensemble. It is the first generative model that solves three problems simultaneously: how does a molecule fold, what is its conformational ensemble, and how does the conformational ensemble change as we change physical temperature. As a concrete test case, we apply Polyformer to protein domains with 50-111 residues and report good agreement of model predictions to Molecular Dynamics (MD) trajectories.

</details>


### [440] [PUFFIN: Protein Unit Discovery with Functional Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14796)
*Gökçe Uludoğan,Buse Giledereli,Elif Ozkirimli,Arzucan Özgür*

Main category: q-bio.BM

TL;DR: The paper introduces PUFFIN, a data-driven framework that identifies protein units by combining structural partitioning with functional supervision using graph neural networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitation of existing methods that either focus solely on residue-level signals, curated annotations, or structure segmentation without integrating functional information.

Method: PUFFIN uses residue-level structure graphs and applies a graph neural network with a structure-aware pooling mechanism to partition proteins into functionally coherent multi-residue units.

Result: PUFFIN's protein units are structurally coherent, exhibit relationships with molecular functions, and align with curated InterPro annotations.

Conclusion: PUFFIN offers a novel, interpretable framework for exploring protein structure-function relationships by identifying coherent protein units with functional associations, advancing our understanding of protein biology.

Abstract: Proteins carry out biological functions through the coordinated action of groups of residues organized into structural arrangements. These arrangements, which we refer to as protein units, exist at an intermediate scale, being larger than individual residues yet smaller than entire proteins. A deeper understanding of protein function can be achieved by identifying these units and their associations with function. However, existing approaches either focus on residue-level signals, rely on curated annotations, or segment protein structures without incorporating functional information, thereby limiting interpretable analysis of structure-function relationships. We introduce PUFFIN, a data-driven framework for discovering protein units by jointly learning structural partitioning and functional supervision. PUFFIN represents proteins as residue-level structure graphs and applies a graph neural network with a structure-aware pooling mechanism that partitions each protein into multi-residue units, with functional supervision that shapes the partition. We show that the learned units are structurally coherent, exhibit organized associations with molecular function, and show meaningful correspondence with curated InterPro annotations. Together, these results demonstrate that PUFFIN provides an interpretable framework for analyzing structure-function relationships using learned protein units and their statistical function associations. We made our source code available at https://github.com/boun-tabi-lifelu/puffin.

</details>


<div id='cs.GT'></div>

# cs.GT [[Back]](#toc)

### [441] [CoopEval: Benchmarking Cooperation-Sustaining Mechanisms and LLM Agents in Social Dilemmas](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.15267)
*Emanuel Tewolde,Xiao Zhang,David Guzman Piedrahita,Vincent Conitzer,Zhijing Jin*

Main category: cs.GT

TL;DR: This paper evaluates game-theoretic mechanisms like repetition, reputation systems, mediation, and contracts to improve cooperation between LLMs in mixed-motive scenarios. Contracting and mediation show the highest effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent advanced LLMs show less cooperative behavior in social dilemmas; addressing this is critical for safety and effective interaction with other agents.

Method: Comparative study of four cooperation mechanisms across multiple social dilemmas, assessing their ability to drive robust cooperation between rational LLM agents.

Result: Contract-based agreements and third-party mediators are most effective in fostering cooperation; repetition fails when players vary, but all mechanisms perform better under evolutionary payoff pressure.

Conclusion: Robust cooperation can be achieved in LLMs using selective mechanisms like contracting and mediation, ensuring safer and more rational interaction in mixed-motive scenarios.

Abstract: It is increasingly important that LLM agents interact effectively and safely with other goal-pursuing agents, yet, recent works report the opposite trend: LLMs with stronger reasoning capabilities behave _less_ cooperatively in mixed-motive games such as the prisoner's dilemma and public goods settings. Indeed, our experiments show that recent models -- with or without reasoning enabled -- consistently defect in single-shot social dilemmas.
  To tackle this safety concern, we present the first comparative study of game-theoretic mechanisms that are designed to enable cooperative outcomes between rational agents _in equilibrium_. Across four social dilemmas testing distinct components of robust cooperation, we evaluate the following mechanisms: (1) repeating the game for many rounds, (2) reputation systems, (3) third-party mediators to delegate decision making to, and (4) contract agreements for outcome-conditional payments between players. Among our findings, we establish that contracting and mediation are most effective in achieving cooperative outcomes between capable LLM models, and that repetition-induced cooperation deteriorates drastically when co-players vary. Moreover, we demonstrate that these cooperation mechanisms become _more effective_ under evolutionary pressures to maximize individual payoffs.

</details>


### [442] [Coalition Formation in LLM Agent Networks: Stability Analysis and Convergence Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14386)
*Dongxin Guo,Jikun Wu,Siu-Ming Yiu*

Main category: cs.GT

TL;DR: The paper introduces a formal framework for understanding coalition formation among LLM agents, grounded in hedonic game theory, and validates its approach through experiments with models like GPT-4.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of theoretical characterization of coalition formation in LLM agents operating in multi-agent systems, and to establish a stable and theoretical foundation for such systems.

Method: The authors propose the LLM Coalition Formation Game (LCFG) grounded in hedonic game theory, deriving conditions for Nash-stable partitions, proving complexity results, and validating their approach through empirical experiments with different LLMs and prompting methods.

Result: The experiments across 2,400 episodes show that LLM coalitions achieve Nash stability most frequently (73.2%) under their Coalition-of-Thought protocol, outperforming other prompting methods.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully formalizes coalition formation in LLM systems, demonstrates significant improvements in stability with specific protocols, and provides a robust foundation for designing multi-agent LLM systems.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents are increasingly deployed in multi-agent systems requiring strategic coordination. While recent work has analyzed LLM behavior in two-player games, coalition formation, where $n$ agents dynamically form cooperative groups, remains theoretically uncharacterized. We present the first framework grounding coalition formation in LLM agent networks in hedonic game theory with formal stability guarantees. We introduce the LLM Coalition Formation Game (LCFG), establish sufficient conditions for Nash-stable partitions, and prove complexity results. Our analysis reveals that LLM agents exhibit bounded rationality characterized by $ε$-rational preferences; we provide both deterministic existence guarantees and consistency-driven stability bounds whose predictions are consistent with empirical outcomes. Experiments with GPT-4, Claude-3, and Llama-3 across 2,400 episodes validate our framework: LLM coalitions achieve Nash stability in 73.2% of cases under our Coalition-of-Thought (CoalT) protocol, compared to 58.4% under chain-of-thought and 41.8% under standard prompting ($p < 0.001$). Our framework provides theoretical foundations for designing stable multi-agent LLM systems.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [443] [An Edge-Cloud Collaborative Architecture for Proactive Elderly Care: Real-Time Risk Assessment and Three-Level Emergency Response](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14154)
*Lijie Zhou,Luran Wang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper presents an edge-cloud collaborative healthcare monitoring system for elderly care, focusing on real-time risk assessment, multi-modal sensor fusion, and privacy preservation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges of existing cloud-based elderly monitoring systems, such as high latency, privacy risks, and limited alert mechanisms, especially under emergency conditions.

Method: Developed a five-layer edge-cloud architecture employing multi-modal sensor fusion, a four-dimensional risk model, and dynamic emergency response tiers. Evaluated on standard datasets and tested on Raspberry Pi hardware.

Result: Achieved 91% activity recognition accuracy, 84% anomaly detection F1-score, and under 100 ms inference latency on edge devices.

Conclusion: The proposed system enhances real-time, privacy-respecting, and efficient monitoring, representing a significant step forward in elderly care technology.

Abstract: The rapid aging of global populations has created an urgent need for intelligent healthcare monitoring systems to ensure the safety of elderly individuals living independently. Existing cloud-centric platforms face critical limitations, including high latency unsuitable for emergency response, privacy risks from continuous transmission of sensitive data, and limited, single-channel alert mechanisms lacking scalability and context awareness. This paper proposes an edge-cloud collaborative architecture that addresses these challenges through real-time multi-modal sensor fusion, a four-dimensional risk assessment model, and a three-level emergency response system. The framework adopts a five-layer design - device, edge, service, data, and application layers - enabling real-time risk evaluation with end-to-end alert latency under three seconds. At the edge, a weighted multi-modal fusion algorithm integrates data from five sensor types with confidence propagation. A unified risk score is generated by combining fall probability, physiological indicators, behavioral patterns, and sensor anomaly metrics. Based on dynamic thresholds, a three-tier notification system coordinates responses among family members, community doctors, and nearby volunteers. Experiments on CASAS, MIMIC-III, and SisFall datasets show that the approach achieves 91% activity recognition accuracy and an 84% anomaly detection F1-score, outperforming single-sensor methods. Deployment on Raspberry Pi 4 gateways demonstrates sub-100 ms inference latency while preserving privacy by keeping raw data local. This architecture advances practical, privacy-preserving, and responsive elderly care systems.

</details>


### [444] [Temporal Cross-Modal Knowledge-Distillation-Based Transfer-Learning for Gas Turbine Vibration Fault Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14766)
*Ali Bagheri Nejad,Mahdi Aliyari-Shoorehdeli,Abolfazl Hasanzadeh*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper introduces the TCMKDTL framework, which combines knowledge distillation and transfer learning for efficient early fault detection in industrial equipment, especially gas turbines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to enhance early fault detection in gas turbines by addressing the limitations of modern deep learning models, such as operational constraints, lack of temporal signal context, and data scarcity.

Method: The proposed framework uses a privileged teacher model trained on broad temporal contexts to transfer knowledge to a smaller student model. It combines pre-training on benchmark datasets and domain adaptation for industrial applications.

Result: The framework improves feature separability and diagnostic accuracy, outperforming traditional pre-trained architectures in experiments using gas turbine datasets.

Conclusion: TCMKDTL enables effective unsupervised anomaly detection for industrial hardware under resource constraints, advancing machine fault prevention strategies.

Abstract: Preventing machine failure is inherently superior to reactive remediation, particularly for critical assets like gas turbines, where early fault detection (FD) is a cornerstone of industrial sustainability. However, modern deep learning-based FD models often face a significant trade-off between architectural complexity and real-time operational constraints, often hindered by a lack of temporal context within restricted vibration signal windows. To address these challenges, this study proposes a Temporal Cross-Modal Knowledge-Distillation Transfer-Learning (TCMKDTL) framework. The framework employs a "privileged" teacher model trained on expansive temporal windows incorporating both past and future signal context to distill latent feature-based knowledge into a compact student model. To mitigate issues of data scarcity and domain shift, the framework leverages robust pre-training on benchmark datasets (such as CWRU) followed by adaptation to target industrial data. Extensive evaluation using experimental and industrial gas turbine (MGT-40) datasets demonstrates that TCMKDTL achieves superior feature separability and diagnostic accuracy compared to conventional pre-trained architectures. Ultimately, this approach enables high-performance, unsupervised anomaly detection suitable for deployment on resource-constrained industrial hardware.

</details>


<div id='cs.CE'></div>

# cs.CE [[Back]](#toc)

### [445] [Decoupling Identity from Utility: Privacy-by-Design Frameworks for Financial Ecosystems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14495)
*Ifayoyinsola Ibikunle,Tyler Farnan,Senthil Kumar,Mayana Pereira*

Main category: cs.CE

TL;DR: This paper examines two Privacy-by-Design methods—Differentially Private Synthetic Data and DP-Seeded Agent-Based Modeling (ABM)—to enhance data utility while ensuring regulatory-compliant privacy protections in financial analytics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the trade-off financial institutions face between maximizing data utility and minimizing re-identification risks in traditional anonymization methods, compounded by stringent regulatory demands.

Method: The study explores two generative paradigms: Direct Tabular Synthesis, which captures joint distributions from raw data for static analyses, and DP-Seeded Agent-Based Modeling, which uses DP-protected aggregates for dynamic, forward-looking simulations.

Result: The paper finds that tabular synthesis is optimal for replicating historical data patterns for QA and analytics, while DP-Seeded ABM excels in simulating dynamic behaviors, facilitating cross-institutional research and resilient decision-making.

Conclusion: The proposed privacy-preserving methods effectively balance data utility and privacy, bypassing traditional anonymization issues and supporting compliance with evolving regulations, making them valuable for financial institutions managing sensitive data.

Abstract: Financial institutions face tension between maximizing data utility and mitigating the re-identification risks inherent in traditional anonymization methods. This paper explores Differentially Private (DP) synthetic data as a robust "Privacy by Design" framework to resolve this conflict, ensuring output privacy while satisfying stringent regulatory obligations. We examine two distinct generative paradigms: Direct Tabular Synthesis, which reconstructs high-fidelity joint distributions from raw data, and DP-Seeded Agent-Based Modeling (ABM), which uses DP-protected aggregates to parameterize complex, stateful simulations. While tabular synthesis excels at reflecting static historical correlations for QA testing and business analytics, the DP-Seeded ABM offers a forward-looking "counterfactual laboratory" capable of modeling dynamic market behaviors and black swan events. By decoupling individual identities from data utility, these methodologies eliminate traditional data-clearing bottlenecks, enabling seamless cross-institutional research and compliant decision-making in an evolving regulatory landscape.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.QM'></div>

# q-bio.QM [[Back]](#toc)

### [446] [Mamba-SSM with LLM Reasoning for Biomarker Discovery: Causal Feature Refinement via Chain-of-Thought Gene Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14334)
*Pushpa Kumar Balan,Aijing Feng*

Main category: q-bio.QM

TL;DR: This paper proposes using LLM chain-of-thought reasoning to filter tissue-composition confounders from gene lists produced by gradient saliency in deep sequence models, leading to improved classifier performance with fewer features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the issue of tissue-composition confounders contaminating gene lists derived from gradient saliency, which undermines downstream classifier performance and reliable biological interpretation.

Method: The authors trained a Mamba SSM on TCGA-BRCA RNA-seq data, extracted a 50-gene list using gradient saliency, and utilized an LLM (DeepSeek-R1) with structured chain-of-thought reasoning to filter the list down to a final 17 genes.

Result: The LLM-filtered 17-gene set achieved an AUC of 0.927, outperforming both the raw 50-gene saliency set (AUC 0.832) and a 5,000-gene variance baseline (AUC 0.903), while employing a significantly reduced number of features.

Conclusion: Targeted removal of confounders via LLM reasoning improves downstream classifier performance, but the incomplete biological faithfulness (e.g., missing key BRCA genes) highlights a gap between empirical performance improvements and faithful biomarker selection.

Abstract: Gradient saliency from deep sequence models surfaces candidate biomarkers efficiently, but the resulting gene lists are contaminated by tissue-composition confounders that degrade downstream classifiers. We study whether LLM chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning can faithfully filter these confounders, and whether reasoning quality drives downstream performance. We train a Mamba SSM on TCGA-BRCA RNA-seq and extract the top-50 genes by gradient saliency; DeepSeek-R1 evaluates every candidate with structured CoT to produce a final 17-gene set. The raw 50-gene saliency set (no LLM) performs worse than a 5,000-gene variance baseline (AUC 0.832 vs. 0.903), while the LLM-filtered set surpasses it (AUC 0.927), using 294x fewer features. A faithfulness audit (COSMIC CGC, OncoKB, PAM50) reveals only 6 of 17 selected genes (35.3%) are validated BRCA biomarkers, yet 10 of 16 known BRCA genes in the input were missed - including FOXA1. This gap between downstream performance and reasoning faithfulness suggests selective faithfulness: targeted confounder removal is sufficient for performance gains even without comprehensive recall.

</details>


<div id='cs.IR'></div>

# cs.IR [[Back]](#toc)

### [447] [Controlling Authority Retrieval: A Missing Retrieval Objective for Authority-Governed Knowledge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14488)
*Andre Bacellar*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: This paper introduces Controlling Authority Retrieval (CAR), a formalized method for identifying the active frontier of authoritative documents, validated across multiple domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need to effectively recover active relevant documents in knowledge systems where formal authority evolves over time, such as in law, drug regulation, and software security.

Method: The authors define the relevant problem mathematically as CAR and propose theoretical results (Theorem 4 and Proposition 2) along with testing their effectiveness on real-world datasets.

Result: Empirical validation using real-world corpora such as security advisories, SCOTUS cases, and FDA records demonstrated CAR's promised applicability and highlighted improvements in recall metrics through the proposed methods.

Conclusion: The CAR framework proves effective in addressing authority-driven retrieval tasks, with significant accuracy improvements over naive approaches. Released datasets and tools enhance the reproducibility and practical application of this research.

Abstract: In any domain where knowledge accumulates under formal authority -- law, drug regulation, software security -- a later document can formally void an earlier one while remaining semantically distant from it. We formalize this as Controlling Authority Retrieval (CAR): recovering the active frontier front(cl(A_k(q))) of the authority closure of the semantic anchor set -- a different mathematical problem from argmax_d s(q,d). The two central results are: Theorem 4 (CAR-Correctness Characterization) gives necessary-and-sufficient conditions on any retrieved set R for TCA(R,q)=1 -- frontier inclusion and no-ignored-superseder -- independent of how R was produced. Proposition 2 (Scope Identifiability Upper Bound) establishes phi(q) as a hard worst-case ceiling: for any scope-indexed algorithm, TCA@k <= phi(q) * R_anchor(q), proved by an adversarial permutation argument. Three independent real-world corpora validate the proved structure: security advisories (Dense TCA@5=0.270, two-stage 0.975), SCOTUS overruling pairs (Dense=0.172, two-stage 0.926), FDA drug records (Dense=0.064, two-stage 0.774). A GPT-4o-mini experiment shows the downstream cost: Dense RAG produces explicit "not patched" claims for 39% of queries where a patch exists; Two-Stage cuts this to 16%. Four benchmark datasets, domain adapters, and a single-command scorer are released at https://github.com/andremir/car-retrieval.

</details>


### [448] [Don't Retrieve, Navigate: Distilling Enterprise Knowledge into Navigable Agent Skills for QA and RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14572)
*Yiqun Sun,Pengfei Wei,Lawrence B. Hsieh*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: Corpus2Skill enhances Retrieval-Augmented Generation by creating a hierarchical skill directory for better corpus navigation by LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations of traditional Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) methods, which treat the model as a passive consumer of search results and fail to leverage the organization of the corpus or account for scattered evidence.

Method: The paper proposes Corpus2Skill, a pipeline that clusters documents and generates a hierarchical directory of LLM-written summaries, enabling agents to navigate the corpus more effectively during retrieval and reasoning.

Result: Corpus2Skill outperforms existing methods, including dense retrieval, RAPTOR, and agentic RAG baselines, on the WixQA benchmark across all quality metrics.

Conclusion: This approach empowers LLMs by providing explicit hierarchical structure and reasoning ability, thereby improving their performance in evidence-based tasks.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) grounds LLM responses in external evidence but treats the model as a passive consumer of search results: it never sees how the corpus is organized or what it has not yet retrieved, limiting its ability to backtrack or combine scattered evidence. We present Corpus2Skill, which distills a document corpus into a hierarchical skill directory offline and lets an LLM agent navigate it at serve time. The compilation pipeline iteratively clusters documents, generates LLM-written summaries at each level, and materializes the result as a tree of navigable skill files. At serve time, the agent receives a bird's-eye view of the corpus, drills into topic branches via progressively finer summaries, and retrieves full documents by ID. Because the hierarchy is explicitly visible, the agent can reason about where to look, backtrack from unproductive paths, and combine evidence across branches. On WixQA, an enterprise customer-support benchmark for RAG, Corpus2Skill outperforms dense retrieval, RAPTOR, and agentic RAG baselines across all quality metrics.

</details>


### [449] [PriHA: A RAG-Enhanced LLM Framework for Primary Healthcare Assistant in Hong Kong](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.14215)
*Richard Wai Cheung Chan,Shanru Lin,Ya-nan Ma,Hao Chen,Liangjun Jiang,Wenqi Fan*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The authors propose a system called PriHA to improve public health self-management in Hong Kong. It utilizes a Retrieval-Augmented Generation-enhanced pipeline to provide accurate healthcare information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study addresses the growing expenses in public health and aims to improve information accessibility for self-health management by using AI technologies.
